macro_segments.title: Introduction and Context: Return to the Lottery Discussion from Yoma 24b

micro_segments.title: Overview of daf 25 and transition from prior discussion on Yoma 24b about avoda and non-kohanim
Rekindling the discussion: the lottery (payis) for Trumat Hadeshen and temple hierarchy

macro_segments.title: Why Multiple Lotteries? Rabbi Yochanan's Teaching on the Excitement of Multiple Drawings

micro_segments.title: Question: Why do they perform the payis four separate times instead of once at the beginning of the day?
Rabbi Yochanan's answer: to create commotion (ragesh) in the entire courtyard; proof from Psalms 26:6
Discussion of pshat versus drash in the verse; the juxtaposition of sod (secret) and ragesh (commotion)

macro_segments.title: The Debate: In What Garments Do the Kohanim Perform the Lottery?

micro_segments.title: Rav Nachman versus Rav Sheshet: weekday garments (bigdei chol) or priestly garments (bigdei kehunah)?
Rav Nachman's reasoning: avoiding bullies who would grab the service without proper change of garments
Rav Sheshet's counter-reasoning: the winner might run eagerly without proper change if not already dressed
Rav Nachman's source: the Mishnah in Tamid describing the stripping and clothing process
Rav Huna bar Yehuda on behalf of Rav Sheshet: the mishna refers to those who did NOT win
The problem of the michnasayim (undergarments): must be put directly on flesh per the verse
Proposed solution: putting on priestly undergarment beneath the weekday cloak before removing the cloak

macro_segments.title: The Priestly Headgear (Mitznefet) and Rav Sheshet's Evidence from the Chamber of Hewn Stone

micro_segments.title: If they wore weekday garments, how could they have had a mitznefet (priestly head covering)?
Rav Yehuda's teaching: a kohen's mother could make him a ketonet to wear in performing his role
Rashi's explanation: mothers made beautiful priestly-looking garments that were not actually bigdei kehunah

macro_segments.title: The Priestly Headgear (Mitznefet) and Rav Sheshet's Evidence from the Chamber of Hewn Stone (continued)

micro_segments.title: Description of the Lishkat Hagazit (Chamber of Hewn Stone): shaped like a basilica with specific spatial and architectural features
The lottery ceremony: appointment of the lottery director (memuneh), removal of headgear from one kohen, counting begins from that point

macro_segments.title: The Chamber's Divided Status: Half in Kodesh, Half Outside; Two Doors and the Pasuk

micro_segments.title: Abaaye's inference: the chamber must be half in the Azarah and half outside to accommodate both the lottery and the Sanhedrin's seating
Problem: only kings of Davidic house can sit in the Azarah; yet the elder of the court sat in the chamber
Problem: the pasuk 'Beis Elohim nehalech beragesh' requires the lottery to occur in the Kodesh (Azarah proper)
Solution: two doors—one opening to Kodesh (Azarah), one opening to Chol (non-Azarah)—define the boundary status
Principle: chambers built in one space but opening into another take their sanctity from where the door opens
Tosafot's distinction: sanctity of the space vs. actual location; the pasuk's concern is sanctity, not physical grounds
Rabbenu Tam's answer: inappropriate to stand in the Azarah with head uncovered when removing the mitznefet
Rav Yitzchak's answer: arranged in a circle meant some would have their backs to the Heichal—improper in the open Azarah

macro_segments.title: The Second Lottery: Distribution of Tamid Services Among Thirteen Kohanim

micro_segments.title: The thirteen assigned tasks of the tamid service: shochet, zorek, dashen inner altar, dashen menora, and carrying the limbs and organs
Ben Azzai's dispute: the order should follow the natural way the animal walks (derech hilucho)
Question: do they perform thirteen separate lotteries or one lottery distributed to thirteen consecutive kohanim?
Resolution: only four gatherings for lotteries total; within each gathering, possibly multiple rounds but evidence of one drawing per service assignment

macro_segments.title: The Ketores Lottery and the Exceptional Case of Combined Tasks

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehuda says no separate lottery for the fire pan (machta); winner of ketores invites the adjacent kohen
Gemara's reasoning: machta and ketores are unified as one service unit, unlike other separate tasks
The prized nature of ketores: tradition that the kohen who performs it becomes rich (segula)
Why no separate lottery despite ketores' prestige: teaching of Rabbi Chiyya establishes the rule that only one lottery per service group

macro_segments.title: The Missing Task: Kabbalat Hadam (Receiving the Blood) and Its Assignment

micro_segments.title: Problem: the mishna lists shochet and zorek but not the kohen who receives (mekabel) the blood
First possibility: the shochet does the kabbalah—rejected because of human nature; he would be impatient for the slaughter
Second possibility: the zorek does the kabbalah—rejected because a non-kohen can perform shechita, risking invalid service
Ben Katin's twelve faucets on the kiyor: enables the twelve tamid kohanim to wash hands simultaneously
Proof from the faucet count: only twelve needed because shechita does not require hand-washing; the mekabel must wash, linking him to zorek
Clinching source from Tamid: 'shachat hashochet v'kibel hamekabel uba lo lizrok'—the one who caught went to throw

topical_tags.term: Tosafot on Sanctity vs. Physical Space
Ben Katin and the Kiyor Faucets
Kidush Yadayim Veraglayim (Hand and Foot Washing)
